Author: Qiliang Yuan <realwujing@gmail.com>

selftests: dhei: Add functional tests for dynamic housekeeping

This commit adds a kselftest suite for Dynamic Housekeeping (DHEI) to verify
sysfs accessibility, safety guards, and SMT-aware isolation. It uses
stress-ng to test load-shedding and tick suppression.

> index 000000000000..a6137c52e713
> --- /dev/null
> +++ b/tools/testing/selftests/dhei/dhei_test.sh
> @@ -0,0 +1,160 @@
> +#!/bin/sh
> +# DHEI (Dynamic Housekeeping & Enhanced Isolation) Full-Coverage Verification Script
> +# Strict POSIX compliant version for reliability on all shells.
> +
> +SYSFS_BASE="/sys/kernel/housekeeping"
> +ONLINE_CPUS=$(cat /sys/devices/system/cpu/online)
> +LAST_CPU=$(echo "$ONLINE_CPUS" | awk -F'[,-]' '{print $NF}')
> +
> +# Colors for output
> +GREEN='\033[0;32m'
> +RED='\033[0;31m'
> +NC='\033[0m'
> +
> +log_pass() { echo "${GREEN}[OK]${NC} $1"; }
> +log_fail() { echo "${RED}[FAIL]${NC} $1"; exit 1; }

This isn't a bug, but POSIX-compliant shells like dash don't consistently
interpret backslash escapes in echo. Should this use printf instead to ensure
the colors format correctly?

The kselftest framework expects a skip code of 4 (KSFT_SKIP) when a test
cannot run because a feature is missing. Does exiting with 1 here cause false
failures in CI pipelines on kernels without DHEI configured?

Also, since this script modifies global sysfs settings and spawns background
jobs like stress-ng, does calling exit 1 directly leak those processes and
leave the system in an unintended isolated state? Could a cleanup trap be
used here to restore state on exit?

[ ... ]

> +test_safety_guard() {
> +    log_info "TEST 2: Safety guard..."
> +    if echo "999-1024" > "$SYSFS_BASE/domain" 2>/dev/null; then

Does this actually verify the DHEI safety guard?

On any system with fewer than 999 CPUs, the generic cpulist_parse() will
reject this string with -EINVAL simply because the IDs are out of bounds.
It seems like this might pass for the wrong reason and fail to test the
actual DHEI safety logic.

> +        log_fail "Safety guard failed: allowed isolation of all CPUs"
> +    fi
> +    log_pass "Safety guard blocked invalid mask"
> +}
> +
> +test_smt_aware_mode() {
> +    log_info "TEST 3: SMT aware logic..."
> +    [ -f /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/topology/thread_siblings_list ] || { log_info "SMT not supported"; return; }
> +    SIBLINGS=$(cat /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/topology/thread_siblings_list)
> +    FIRST=$(echo "$SIBLINGS" | cut -d',' -f1 | cut -d'-' -f1)
> +    echo 1 > "$SYSFS_BASE/smt_aware_mode"
> +    if echo "$FIRST" > "$SYSFS_BASE/timer" 2>/dev/null; then
> +         echo 0 > "$SYSFS_BASE/smt_aware_mode"
> +         log_fail "SMT mode failed: accepted partial core"

Does the presence of thread_siblings_list guarantee SMT is active?

This file exists even on systems without SMT or where it is disabled,
containing only the single thread ID. In that case, writing this single ID
is a full-core isolation, which the kernel correctly accepts, but this
script would misinterpret it as a failure.

> +    else
> +         log_pass "SMT mode correctly rejected partial core"
> +    fi
> +    echo 0 > "$SYSFS_BASE/smt_aware_mode"
> +}
> +
> +get_tick_count() {
> +    grep "LOC:" /proc/interrupts | awk -v cpu="$LAST_CPU" '{print $(cpu+2)}'
> +}

Will this read the correct column if there are offline CPUs?

If any CPU before LAST_CPU is offline, its column is omitted from
/proc/interrupts. This would cause awk to read the wrong column or a string,
breaking the tick suppression test.

[ ... ]

> +test_generic() {
> +    log_info "TEST 5: Notifier propagation..."
> +    for t in rcu workqueue misc kthread managed_irq; do
> +        echo "0-1" > "$SYSFS_BASE/$t"

Do these hardcoded "0-1" masks cause false failures on systems with 1 or 2
CPUs?

On a 1-CPU system, "0-1" is rejected as invalid. On a 2-CPU system, writing
"0-1" puts CPU 1 into the housekeeping mask, which contradicts the later
test_stress_domain expectation where CPU 1 is supposed to be isolated.

Furthermore, this overwrites multiple domains to "0-1" but never restores
them on success, permanently changing the system state.

commit 93bcf88a74470cc1bfc06cd1200fd78cf4759d29
Author: Qiliang Yuan <realwujing@gmail.com>
Date:   Wed Mar 25 09:09:46 2026 +0000

    selftests: dhei: Add functional tests for dynamic housekeeping
    
    Dynamic Housekeeping (DHEI) introduces complex runtime interactions
    across sysfs, scheduler, and various kernel subsystems. There are
    currently no automated tests to verify the integrity of sysfs
    boundaries, safety guards, or SMT-aware isolation logic.
    
    Implement a kselftest suite for DHEI to ensure functional correctness.
    This includes a dedicated test script (dhei_test.sh) covering sysfs
    interface accessibility, safety guard enforcement, and SMT-aware grouping.
    
    The suite also incorporates stress-ng based pressure testing to verify
    load-shedding efficiency on isolated CPUs, Tick suppression under active
    task load, and Workqueue restriction under competitive system pressure.
    
    Usage:
      make -C tools/testing/selftests/dhei run_tests
    
    Signed-off-by: Qiliang Yuan <realwujing@gmail.com>

+

diff --git a/tools/testing/selftests/dhei/dhei_test.sh b/tools/testing/selftests/dhei/dhei_test.sh

--- /dev/null

@@ -0,0 +1,160 @@
+#!/bin/sh
+# DHEI (Dynamic Housekeeping & Enhanced Isolation) Full-Coverage Verification Script
+# Strict POSIX compliant version for reliability on all shells.
+
+SYSFS_BASE="/sys/kernel/housekeeping"
+ONLINE_CPUS=$(cat /sys/devices/system/cpu/online)
+LAST_CPU=$(echo "$ONLINE_CPUS" | awk -F'[,-]' '{print $NF}')
+
+# Colors for output
+GREEN='\033[0;32m'
+RED='\033[0;31m'
+NC='\033[0m'
+
+log_pass() { echo "${GREEN}[OK]${NC} $1"; }
+log_fail() { echo "${RED}[FAIL]${NC} $1"; exit 1; }
+log_info() { echo "[INFO] $1"; }
+
+check_root() {
+    [ "$(id -u)" -eq 0 ] || log_fail "Please run as root"
+}
+
+test_sysfs_structure() {
+    log_info "TEST 1: Sysfs structure..."
+    for node in smt_aware_mode timer rcu misc tick domain workqueue managed_irq kthread; do
+        [ -f "$SYSFS_BASE/$node" ] || log_fail "Node $SYSFS_BASE/$node missing"
+    done
+    log_pass "All 9 DHEI sysfs nodes exist"
+}
+
+test_safety_guard() {
+    log_info "TEST 2: Safety guard..."
+    if echo "999-1024" > "$SYSFS_BASE/domain" 2>/dev/null; then
+        log_fail "Safety guard failed: allowed isolation of all CPUs"
+    fi
+    log_pass "Safety guard blocked invalid mask"
+}
+
+test_smt_aware_mode() {
+    log_info "TEST 3: SMT aware logic..."
+    [ -f /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/topology/thread_siblings_list ] || { log_info "SMT not supported"; return; }
+    SIBLINGS=$(cat /sys/devices/system/cpu/cpu0/topology/thread_siblings_list)
+    FIRST=$(echo "$SIBLINGS" | cut -d',' -f1 | cut -d'-' -f1)
+    echo 1 > "$SYSFS_BASE/smt_aware_mode"
+    if echo "$FIRST" > "$SYSFS_BASE/timer" 2>/dev/null; then
+         echo 0 > "$SYSFS_BASE/smt_aware_mode"
+         log_fail "SMT mode failed: accepted partial core"
+    else
+         log_pass "SMT mode correctly rejected partial core"
+    fi
+    echo 0 > "$SYSFS_BASE/smt_aware_mode"
+}
+
+get_tick_count() {
+    grep "LOC:" /proc/interrupts | awk -v cpu="$LAST_CPU" '{print $(cpu+2)}'
+}
+
+test_tick_dynamic() {
+    log_info "TEST 4: Dynamic Tick toggle..."
+    [ "$LAST_CPU" -eq 0 ] && return
+
+    # Reset all to full housekeeping
+    for node in tick rcu timer domain workqueue; do
+        [ -f "$SYSFS_BASE/$node" ] && echo "$ONLINE_CPUS" > "$SYSFS_BASE/$node" 2>/dev/null
+    done
+
+    S1=$(get_tick_count)
+    sleep 1
+    S2=$(get_tick_count)
+    log_info "Baseline ticks on CPU $LAST_CPU: $((S2-S1)) (per 1s)"
+
+    # Isolate LAST_CPU by setting housekeeping for all types
+    HK_MASK="0-$((LAST_CPU-1))"
+    for node in tick rcu timer domain workqueue; do
+        [ -f "$SYSFS_BASE/$node" ] && echo "$HK_MASK" > "$SYSFS_BASE/$node" 2>/dev/null
+    done
+
+    sleep 1
+    S1=$(get_tick_count)
+    sleep 2
+    S2=$(get_tick_count)
+    DIFF=$((S2-S1))
+    log_info "Tick delta after isolation: $DIFF (per 2s)"
+    [ "$DIFF" -gt 100 ] && log_fail "Tick not suppressed ($DIFF)"
+    log_pass "Tick dynamically suppressed"
+}
+
+test_generic() {
+    log_info "TEST 5: Notifier propagation..."
+    for t in rcu workqueue misc kthread managed_irq; do
+        echo "0-1" > "$SYSFS_BASE/$t"
+        [ "$(cat "$SYSFS_BASE/$t")" = "0-1" ] || log_fail "$t update failed"
+        log_pass "$t verified"
+    done
+}
+
+get_busy() {
+    grep "cpu$LAST_CPU " /proc/stat | awk '{print $2+$3+$4+$7+$8+$9}'
+}
+
+test_stress_domain() {
+    log_info "TEST 6: Stress Domain Isolation..."
+    command -v stress-ng >/dev/null 2>&1 || return
+    [ "$LAST_CPU" -eq 0 ] && return
+    echo "0-1" > "$SYSFS_BASE/domain"
+    stress-ng --cpu 0 --timeout 10 --quiet &
+    PID=$!
+    sleep 2
+    B1=$(get_busy)
+    sleep 5
+    B2=$(get_busy)
+    DIFF=$((B2-B1))
+    log_info "Busy jiffies delta: $DIFF (per 5s)"
+    [ "$DIFF" -gt 150 ] && log_fail "CPU $LAST_CPU not isolated ($DIFF)"
+    log_pass "Domain isolation verified under load"
+    echo "$ONLINE_CPUS" > "$SYSFS_BASE/domain"
+    wait "$PID" 2>/dev/null
+}
+
+test_stress_tick() {
+    log_info "TEST 7: Stress Tick Suppression..."
+    command -v stress-ng >/dev/null 2>&1 || return
+    [ "$LAST_CPU" -eq 0 ] && return
+    echo "$ONLINE_CPUS" > "$SYSFS_BASE/tick"
+    taskset -c "$LAST_CPU" stress-ng --cpu 1 --timeout 15 --quiet &
+    PID=$!
+    sleep 2
+    T1=$(get_tick_count)
+    sleep 2
+    T2=$(get_tick_count)
+    log_info "Ticks WITH housekeeping: $((T2-T1)) (per 2s)"
+
+    echo "0-1" > "$SYSFS_BASE/tick"
+    sleep 2
+    T1=$(get_tick_count)
+    sleep 2
+    T2=$(get_tick_count)
+    DIFF_ISO=$((T2-T1))
+    log_info "Ticks AFTER isolation: $DIFF_ISO (per 2s)"
+
+    # Critical: Check if dmesg shows context tracking warnings during this test
+    [ "$DIFF_ISO" -gt 100 ] && {
+        log_info "Dmesg check for tick errors..."
+        dmesg | grep -i "tick" | tail -n 5
+    }
+
+    log_pass "Tick suppression scenario logged"
+    echo "$ONLINE_CPUS" > "$SYSFS_BASE/tick"
+    wait "$PID" 2>/dev/null
+}
+
+check_root
+test_sysfs_structure
+test_safety_guard
+test_smt_aware_mode
+test_tick_dynamic
+test_generic
+test_stress_domain
+test_stress_tick
+
+log_pass "DHEI Verification Complete!"

Alright, let's break this down. This commit introduces a functional test for Dynamic Housekeeping and Enhanced Isolation, or DHEI, which is something I'm admittedly not intimately familiar with. However, the premise seems straightforward: the test checks the functionality of a new DHEI feature by interacting with `/sys/kernel/housekeeping` files. Initially, the Makefile structure looks correct, using `TEST_PROGS` for the executable script `dhei_test.sh`.

My first immediate thoughts revolve around best practices for selftests. Based on my expertise, several areas require closer scrutiny. The Bash script `dhei_test.sh` is an executable, so that's consistent with the framework. The script performs various checks: it verifies the existence of sysfs nodes, tests domain settings with `stress-ng` (though it quietly skips if `stress-ng` is unavailable, which is reasonable), and does some interrupt count analysis.

However, the major concern starts to crystallize around how the script is architected. A significant red flag is the absence of a check for whether the DHEI feature is even *available* in the current kernel. Currently, the script unconditionally fails (exit 1) if `/sys/kernel/housekeeping` doesn't exist. This is problematic; a proper selftest should `KSFT_SKIP` (exit 4) if a prerequisite feature is missing. The current behaviour will lead to false negatives in CI environments if DHEI is not enabled in the kernel configuration. It's essentially treating a "missing feature" as an outright "failure". That's a fundamental flaw.

A related concern is that the script modifies system state (by writing to `/sys/kernel/housekeeping/*`) without saving the original state or ensuring the state is restored at the end, regardless of success or failure. This leaves the system in an unknown state after the test, which can impact subsequent tests or the overall stability of the system. This breaks a fundamental expectation for these kinds of tests.

A more subtle, but definitely concerning, architectural problem arises in `get_tick_count`. The function extracts CPU-specific interrupt counts from `/proc/interrupts`. It assumes a contiguous and consistent mapping between CPU indexes and the columns in `/proc/interrupts`, as read with `awk`. If CPUs are offline, this assumption breaks down. The script calculates the last online CPU as `LAST_CPU` and then uses it as an index into `/proc/interrupts`. However, the layout of `/proc/interrupts` changes depending on the online CPUs. If there are gaps in online CPUs, the calculation of the correct column fails. This will lead to an incorrect count, or an `awk` error, if there are offline CPUs.

Furthermore, there is a discrepancy in how tick suppression is handled in the script. While one of the tests will fail, another one merely logs a message if the difference in tick counts is above a threshold. This is inconsistent and might hide issues.

1.  **Missing `KSFT_SKIP` for unsupported kernels:** The script fails (exit 1) instead of skipping (exit 4) if the DHEI feature is not enabled.
2.  **System state modification without restoration:** The script modifies system housekeeping settings but does not restore the previous configuration.
3.  **Flawed CPU indexing for `/proc/interrupts`:** The CPU index calculation used for `/proc/interrupts` is buggy when CPUs are offline.

    "thought": "**Summary of Commit Review: DHEI Functional Test**

Okay, here's my assessment of this commit adding functional tests for Dynamic Housekeeping (DHEI). I see a good start with the kselftest suite, but I have some serious concerns about the test logic, especially with CPU handling and sysfs interactions. The tests need a thorough going-over.

First, the script's approach to parsing `/proc/interrupts` is a major red flag. It assumes a fixed column index ( `LAST_CPU+2`) to retrieve the local timer interrupt count. This is brittle. If a CPU is offline *before* the `LAST_CPU`, the column count will be off, leading to incorrect values or even attempting to parse the wrong data (like the interrupt name), resulting in false negatives or silent failures. This column index should be determined dynamically by parsing the CPU headers in the output from `/proc/interrupts`.

Second, the hardcoded CPU masks, specifically the `"0-1"` used in several tests, are problematic. If the system only has a single core, this writes an invalid mask, causing an error and making the `test_generic()` test immediately fail. In the stress-testing scenarios, this mask introduces a fundamental flaw in the test design. With dual-core systems, `"0-1"` means the last CPU is *not* isolated; it's part of the housekeeping, so the tests will always fail because they expect the CPU to be isolated. The tests need to be much smarter about generating appropriate masks based on the number of online CPUs. For the dual core scenario (and single core), tests should ensure the expected mask is properly set. This might involve different branches and setting a more appropriate mask based on the system configuration.

Third, the "safety guard" test doesn't actually test the safety guard at all. It uses a range ("999-1024") that is almost guaranteed to be out-of-bounds on any real system, triggering a basic CPU list parsing error. This test, in its current form, is a false positive and doesn't validate the claimed feature. The test should try to isolate *all* CPUs, or isolate a mask of all *online* CPUs. Only then is it a valid test of the DHEI feature.

Finally, the lack of state restoration on test failure is poor form. If a test fails and `log_fail()` is called, the script exits without cleaning up the state of the housekeeping sysfs nodes. This leaves the system in an unpredictable state – potentially with certain CPUs isolated, interrupts disabled, or other DHEI settings modified. Subsequent tests or normal system operation could be adversely affected. A proper `trap` or cleanup mechanism is essential to ensure that any changes are reverted, no matter how the script exits.

In conclusion, these tests need a revision.

selftests: dhei: Add functional tests for dynamic housekeeping

This commit adds a kselftest suite for Dynamic Housekeeping (DHEI) to verify
sysfs accessibility, safety guards, and SMT-aware isolation. It uses
stress-ng to test load-shedding and tick suppression.

breaking the tick suppression test.

Do these hardcoded "0-1" masks cause false failures on systems with 1 or 2
CPUs?

On a 1-CPU system, "0-1" is rejected as invalid. On a 2-CPU system, writing
"0-1" puts CPU 1 into the housekeeping mask, which contradicts the later
test_stress_domain expectation where CPU 1 is supposed to be isolated.

Furthermore, this overwrites multiple domains to "0-1" but never restores
them on success, permanently changing the system state.
